chunk_summary: This section discusses sodium targets, diuretic monitoring, and fluid restriction strategies in pediatric cirrhosis with ascites, emphasizing tailored sodium management and avoiding routine fluid restriction unless serum sodium is ≤125 mmol/L.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of sodium restriction in managing pediatric cirrhosis with ascites.
Learn when and how to implement fluid restriction based on serum sodium levels.
Identify safe sodium targets and adjust dietary plans based on age, ascites severity, and renal function.
Monitor diuretic use and its impact on weight, serum electrolytes, and renal function.
key_facts: Fluid restriction is recommended only when serum sodium is ≤125 mmol/L.
Sodium targets for cholestasis/cirrhosis range from 1–2 mEq/kg/day but must be adjusted based on individual factors.
Spironolactone is commonly used at 1–6 mg/kg/day as the initial diuretic in pediatric practice.
Strict fluid restriction can reduce energy density and worsen feeding conflict in children.
Monitor weight trajectory, serum sodium, potassium, and renal function regularly during diuretic therapy.
reference_quote: Major cirrhosis guidance supports fluid restriction only when there is moderate to severe hyponatremia. (Source: https://easl.eu/wp-content/uploads/2018/10/decompensated-cirrhosis-English-report.pdf)
document_key_topics: Sodium restriction in pediatric cirrhosis with ascites
Fluid restriction guidelines based on serum sodium levels
Diuretic use and monitoring in pediatric liver disease
Nutritional strategies to balance calorie density and sodium intake
